title: Act I: The Arrival
sourceName: Luigi Pirandello — Six Characters in Search of an Author (1921)
paragraphs: The curtain rises on an empty theater stage. A Company of actors is rehearsing. The MANAGER is directing. Suddenly, six strange figures appear from the back of the auditorium: THE FATHER, THE MOTHER, THE STEPDAUGHTER, THE SON, THE BOY, and THE CHILD. They are dressed in mourning clothes and have an unreal quality—as though they have stepped out of a story.
MANAGER: (irritated) What's going on here? Who are these people?
FATHER: (stepping forward) We are looking for an author.
MANAGER: An author? What author?
FATHER: Any author. It doesn't matter which. We have a story—a real story, a true story—and no one has written us. We exist—we are alive—but we have no author. And so we came here.
MANAGER: (amused despite himself) You—came here. You exist?

FATHER: Yes. We exist. Characters are more real than people, you know. People change every day—they die—they become unrecognizable. Characters are fixed. What we are, we are forever. The Father—(he gestures to himself)—will always be The Father. The Mother will always be The Mother. This suffering—(he gestures to the group)—will always be exactly as it is.
MANAGER: What is your story?

FATHER: It is a story of shame. Of a man—myself—who arranged for his wife and the man she loved to live together because he—I—was unable to give her what she needed. And then—years later—without knowing it—I met my stepdaughter in a certain kind of establishment.
STEPDAUGHTER: (bitterly, interrupting) Say it plainly. In a brothel. He found me in a brothel.
FATHER: (with dignity) I did not know who she was.
STEPDAUGHTER: Does that excuse it?
MANAGER: This is—quite a story.

FATHER: We are not asking you to excuse anyone. We are only asking you to give us form—to let us play out our story so that it exists, so that it has been said. We are characters who have been abandoned by their author. We have a right to live.
MANAGER: (to his actors) What do you think? Shall we try it?
LEADING ACTOR: It's unusual.
MANAGER: Everything unusual is interesting. (To the Father.) Very well. Tell me the story. We'll see if it can be staged.

title: Act II: The Story
sourceName: Luigi Pirandello — Six Characters in Search of an Author (1921)
paragraphs: ACT II

The SIX CHARACTERS attempt to perform their story. The ACTORS try to reproduce what the characters show them. But there is constant friction—the characters insist that the actors cannot capture their truth.

STEPDAUGHTER: (watching the Leading Actress rehearse her role) No—no—it isn't like that. She is doing it all wrong. I didn't cry like that. I wasn't—(She tries to describe it, breaks down, then recovers.) You don't understand. You can't understand, because you are actors. You put on masks. We are the masks—or rather, we are the faces under the masks, and the faces are permanent.

FATHER: (to the Manager) This is the problem with theater: it always substitutes. The thing you represent is never the thing itself. We are the thing itself, and your actors can only represent us—imperfectly, always imperfectly.
MANAGER: That is true of all theater.
FATHER: Yes. But for us it is especially painful, because we know the truth and must watch it be distorted.
The MOTHER has been weeping silently throughout. THE SON stands apart, hostile, wanting nothing to do with any of it.
SON: (finally speaking) I want no part of this. This is our private life—our shame—and you are making it into a spectacle. I refuse to participate.
FATHER: But you have no choice. You are a character. You exist in this story whether you wish to or not.
SON: I refuse.
FATHER: Your refusal is also part of the story.
(The SON turns away. This too is observed and noted.)
The MANAGER tries to reconstruct the scene in the brothel—the moment when the Father found the Stepdaughter.
STEPDAUGHTER: (suddenly stopping) There was a child here. My little sister. Where is she?
(Everyone looks around. THE CHILD has wandered to the back of the stage, near the stage garden.)
THE BOY: (the elder child, staring at something no one else sees) Don't—don't go near the pond—
(THE CHILD falls. They rush to her. She is dead—drowned in an inch of stage water, or by a kind of magic, or by the logic of a story that must reach its end.)
(THE BOY produces a revolver.)
MANAGER: Stop—that's a real—
(A shot.)
(THE BOY falls. Some of the actors run. Others stand frozen.)
MANAGER: Is he—is it real? Is he dead?
(Confusion. Some say yes. Some say no. The question cannot be answered.)

title: Act III: The Question
sourceName: Luigi Pirandello — Six Characters in Search of an Author (1921)
paragraphs: ACT III
The stage is left in confusion. The MANAGER and the ACTORS are shaken. The FATHER and the STEPDAUGHTER have survived—whatever that means for characters.
MANAGER: What just happened?
FATHER: Our story reached one of its endings.
MANAGER: Are the children dead?
FATHER: I don't know. They exist in the story. What happens to characters when their story ends—I cannot say.
LEADING ACTOR: This whole thing was—I don't know what it was. A performance? An event?
FATHER: Does it matter which? It happened. It has now happened, and you have seen it, and whether it was fiction or reality—the experience you had is real. Is it not?
LEADING ACTOR: (uncertain) I suppose—

FATHER: That is what I mean by characters being more real than people. The experience you had just now—that shock, that horror, that confusion—it will stay with you. In fifty years, if you live, you will remember this afternoon. Whereas the play you were rehearsing before we arrived—(he gestures at the script on the table)—you will forget completely.
MANAGER: (sitting down heavily) What am I supposed to make of all this?

FATHER: Make a play of it. (He smiles, just slightly.) Or don't. You have already made something of it, whether you intended to or not. We came here looking for form, and you gave us form. Imperfect—improvised—but form. We have existed, now, in your theater. We will continue to exist in the minds of everyone who was here. That is enough.
(The STEPDAUGHTER laughs—a hard, wild laugh.)
STEPDAUGHTER: Enough? (Still laughing.) Enough for you, Father. It was never enough for me.
(She runs off the stage into the house and laughs from somewhere in the darkness of the theater.)
(The FATHER stands alone on the stage. The MANAGER looks at him. The ACTORS look at each other. The stage lights dim, almost to nothing.)
(In the near-darkness, the shadows of the remaining characters can be seen—hovering, not quite present, not quite absent.)
FATHER: (quietly, to no one in particular) We are here. We have always been here. We were here before you came, and we will be here when you have gone.
(The lights go out.)
CURTAIN.
